coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot-graphene composite: A novel biocide for marine biofouling control in aquaculture cage nets
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ashraf, P.Muhamed
Sahla, V.A.
Anjana, C.S.
Binsi, P.K.
coredata.dc:description:
Recently, heightened concern and reluctance among planners and fishers about using inorganic biocides in aquatic systems have increased the importance of carbon-based nanomaterials, which are free from metallic contaminants and non-toxic to the environment and biota. The study aimed to synthesize carbon dot (CD) from fish eye and to fabricate a CD-graphene composite to test its efficacy as biocide against marine biofouling. The organic components of fish eyes were hydrothermally transformed into fluorescent nano carbon dots as evidenced by their FTIR signatures, fluorescence and UV-Visible spectra. The fish eye CD-graphene composite synthesized hydrothermally and was studied for its chemical and morphological characteristics. A nano-sized CD-graphene composite is formed with a few disorganized layers exhibiting an amorphous nature. Different concentrations of CD-graphene were coated onto surface-modified polyethylene aquaculture cage nets using polyaniline and exposed to the marine environment for 8 months to evaluate the composite's effectiveness as an antifouling agent. The treated cage net exhibited excellent biofouling resistance and the optimum concentration of CD-graphene for efficient resistance was 0.02 %. The study attributed the biocidal mechanism of the composites to the synergistic activity of CDs and graphene under sunlight, which generates ROS and free radical species. This process disrupts microbial biofilm formation on cage nets, leading to enhanced biofouling resistance.
Synopsis The synthesized fish eye waste derived carbon dot-graphene composite, offers a non-toxic, metal-free antifouling solution for aquaculture systems, effectively reducing marine biofouling through sunlight-driven reactive oxygen species and free radical generation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fish eye
CD-graphene composite
Marine biofouling
Aquaculture
Hydrothermal synthesis
Polyaniline surface modification

CD-graphene coating over aquaculture cage nets
Synthesis of CD and its characteristics

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors sincerely thank the Director, ICAR- Central Institute of Fisheries Technology and Head of Fishing Technology, and Microbiology, Fermentation and Biotechnology Division for providing facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) UV-Visible spectrum of fish eye CD, and b) Fluorescence spectra offish eye CD of λex 220–400 nm.
a) Atomic force micrograph and b) Transmission electron micrographs of nano CD.

a) Transmission Electron Micrographs with SAED pattern of CD- graphene composite and b) Atomic Force Microscope topography CD-graphene composite. The axis values of AFM were highlighted for making the values readable.
a) FTIR spectra of CD –graphene with finger printed region, and b) X-ray diffraction pattern of CD-graphene composite.

a) The bar diagram depicts the biofouling accumulation in g /g of net. The result is an average of 4 replication of samples. b) Stereo micrographs of biofouling affected cage net with 25X magnification arranged sequentially on monthly basis viz, 1, 2, and 4 months. Each months depicted untreated control (H0), 0.02 % CD-graphene (H2) and 0.04 % CD- graphene (H3) composite treated nets.
Scanning electron micrographs of CD-graphene coated PE-PANI aquaculture cage net.
FTIR absorptions of fish eye CD and CD-graphene composite. The FTIR absorptions of CD was compared with previously reported data.

entry.bold: FTIR absorption of CD in the present study

N-H str
N-H symm str aliphatic Primary amine

head.ce:title: Carbon dot-graphene composite: A novel biocide for marine biofouling control in aquaculture cage nets

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: ICAR Central Institute of Fisheries Technology, Cochin 682029, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: ICAR Central Institute of Fisheries Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: ICAR Central Institute of Fisheries Technology Cochin 682 029, Kerala India.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: ICAR Central Institute of Fisheries Technology, Matsyapuri PO, Cochin 682029, Kerala, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: ICAR Central Institute of Fisheries Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, heightened concern and reluctance among planners and fishers about using inorganic biocides in aquatic systems have increased the importance of carbon-based nanomaterials, which are free from metallic contaminants and non-toxic to the environment and biota. The study aimed to synthesize carbon dot (CD) from fish eye and to fabricate a CD-graphene composite to test its efficacy as biocide against marine biofouling. The organic components of fish eyes were hydrothermally transformed into fluorescent nano carbon dots as evidenced by their FTIR signatures, fluorescence and UV-Visible spectra. The fish eye CD-graphene composite synthesized hydrothermally and was studied for its chemical and morphological characteristics. A nano-sized CD-graphene composite is formed with a few disorganized layers exhibiting an amorphous nature. Different concentrations of CD-graphene were coated onto surface-modified polyethylene aquaculture cage nets using polyaniline and exposed to the marine environment for 8 months to evaluate the composite's effectiveness as an antifouling agent. The treated cage net exhibited excellent biofouling resistance and the optimum concentration of CD-graphene for efficient resistance was 0.02 %. The study attributed the biocidal mechanism of the composites to the synergistic activity of CDs and graphene under sunlight, which generates ROS and free radical species. This process disrupts microbial biofilm formation on cage nets, leading to enhanced biofouling resistance.

The synthesized fish eye waste derived carbon dot-graphene composite, offers a non-toxic, metal-free antifouling solution for aquaculture systems, effectively reducing marine biofouling through sunlight-driven reactive oxygen species and free radical generation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Marine biofouling poses a significant challenge to aquaculture farmers, as its management accounts for 25 % of the project budget. Biofouling is essentially a chain reaction which begins with the adsorption of organic molecules on the material surface that supports further microbial adhesion and subsequent biofilm formation. These biofilms in turn act as a suitable sticky attachment substratum for larger fouling organisms, such as barnacles and mussels. The need of biofouling mitigation measures spans across various industries including aquaculture industry. In aquaculture cages, biofouling blocks the lumens of the cage nets, leading to the accumulation of unused feed, higher load of microorganisms, chances of algal blooms incidents and ultimately, poor growth and loss of crops (Fitridge et al., 2012; Cornejo et al., 2020). This essentially demands a comprehensive management approach, primarily intervening the initial phases of biofilm formation so as to block the propagation of biofouling chain.

Ashraf et al. (2020) developed an in situ methodology to modify the non polar surface of polyethylene aquaculture cage nets using a conducting polymer, polyaniline. Nano copper oxide was used as a biocide to treat the polyaniline modified PE surface and the same material, under field and laboratory evaluation, exhibited excellent biofouling resistance (Ashraf et al., 2023). Nano copper and zinc analogues were used extensively reinforced in coatings to protect submerged marine objects and has advantages and disadvantages (Miller et al., 2020; Lagerström et al., 2020). However, in recent years there is greater concern and reluctance among planners and fishers regarding the use of inorganic materials as biocide in the aquatic systems. Carbon based nanomaterials are gaining importance in this direction since they are free from metallic contaminants, and are non-toxic to environment and biota.

The emerging carbon based nano materials are graphene and carbon dots (CD) (Campuzano et al., 2019; Anand et al., 2019), however both are not individually capable to work as biocide in the aggressive marine environment. Literature has demonstrated the antibacterial activity of graphene by inducing oxidative and membrane stress through reactive oxygen species (ROS), electron transfer to graphene from the membrane upon contact, and physical damage caused by the sharp edges of graphene (Pinto et al., 2013; Smith et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2015). Tu et al. (2013) showed that graphene induced degradation of both the outer and inner membrane of E coli, affecting its viability, through direct insertion into the cell membrane and phospholipid extraction. On the other hand, carbon dots (CD) are fluorescent zero-dimensional nanomaterials with inherent structural defects and functional groups such as OH, NH, CO etc. They are highly soluble in water, exhibit excellent photo stability and low cytotoxicity (Zhang et al., 2019). CDs are excellent light harvesters in the UV-near IR region and have a considerable photodynamic effect (Juzenas et al., 2013; Kleinauskas et al., 2013; Ge et al., 2016; Lan et al., 2018). N doped CDs/ graphitic carbon nitride successfully used for microbial degradation of nitrate and oxytetracyclin in aquatic system (Peng et al., 2024). Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are generated in the presence of water and air through photo induction, causing oxidative stress that damage microbial cell membrane. This leads to the disruption of DNA/RNA, and results in erratic gene expression, ultimately leading to protein inhibition (Ipe et al., 2005; Li et al., 2016; Awak et al., 2017; Jhonsi et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018).

The marine environment is very harsh and therefore, there is a need to prepare a composite with a special emphasis on organic based one. A composite of CDs and nano graphene is one of the best options, with the expectation of synergistic influences of both. Previously, fabrication of hybrids of graphene and electron withdrawing or releasing molecules showed significant changes in the conductivity due to the conical structure of graphene (Alzate et al., 2020). The composites formed by graphene with metal oxides, organo metallic, and polymers will improve the sensitivity of graphene and it was explained due to the transformation of bonding of graphene from sp2 to sp3 (Alzate et al., 2020). Also major interaction expected through hydrogen bonding, π−π or van der Waals forces. CDs having conjugated CC bonds and other functional groups make them an ideal choice to fabricate CD-hybrids. Strong interaction is expected between CD and graphene due to the multiple bonding facilitated through non covalent interaction, by preserving the characteristics intrinsic electron transport mechanism of graphene (Alzate et al., 2020). Fluorescent CDs extensively employed for non-protein biothiols detection, and this has a significant impact in biomedical research (Shellaiah and Sun, 2023). A graphene sensor decorated with CDs showed improved sensitivity, response and enhanced limit of detection compared to the control graphene field effect transistor (Ramadan et al., 2021), emphasizing the increased conductivity and specificity by the introduction of CD.

In contrast to the reported studies in the literature, the present study is characterised by several key points. The synthesis of a composite biocide of CDs and graphene was carried out using hydrothermal method, which is a green synthesis protocol. Carbon dots (CDs) were derived from fish processing discards, more specifically from fish eye. Furthermore, the biofouling inhibition characteristics of CD-graphene composite was assessed on aquaculture cage nets under actual marine environment. The rationale behind choosing fish eye as the source of carbon dots is that, fish eye contains two major visual pigments, viz. rhodopsin and phorphyropsin (Greiling and Clark, 2008; Mahler et al., 2013). These pigments are rich in conjugated double bonded CC system, which are likely to undergo cyclization and bond reorientation during hydrothermal carbonization. This process may result in the formation of carbon dots with a high propensity for inducing oxidative stress. This study is a maiden attempt to utilize the CD-graphene as an antifouling agent with its effectiveness tested in real field environment.

Rohu fish was procured from the local fish markets of Cochin, India, and the eyes were removed after cleaning the fish using tap water and kept in freezer at −18 °C till analysis. The eye and accompanied muscles were used as such. Acetic acid AR purchased from Central Drug House, India and Milli Q type 1 water, hydrothermal reactor and Nebertherm B180 muffle furnace were used for the synthesis of carbon dots. Graphene platelets of thickness 1–4 nm, purity 91 % were purchased from Reinste Nano Ventures New Delhi, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CD from fish eye
ce:section.ce:para: About 1.5 g of fish eye was digested with 10 ml of 1:1 acetic acid (v/v) solution using a teflon lined hydrothermal reactor. Temperature was programmed to raise at 4 °C per minute increment to 180 °C and the highest temperature was maintained for 7 h. The digest obtained was filtered through 3 layered Whatman 40 filter paper and the filtrate was dialysed using a 12 K Dalton (Sigma Aldrich, Singapore) layer. The CD was evaporated to low volume and kept in refrigerator at 4 °C until further use. Polyethylene aquaculture cage net having 3 × 3 ply, 3.0 cm mesh size and 0.07 cm thickness was purchased from Matsyafed Net factory, India.

Different ratios of fish eye CD and graphene platelets were dispersed in 25 ml water in a hydrothermal reactor. The reactor was heated from RT to 180 °C in 1 h, and maintained at 180 °C for 3 hrs. After cooling, the composite residue was collect dby centrifugation and dried by keeping in a hot air oven at 60 °C for 5 h. The composite was used for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CD-graphene coating over aquaculture cage nets
ce:section.ce:para: Polyethylene is the main polymer used for preparation of aquaculture cage nets. The non-polar surface of polyethylene net was modified using polyaniline, a conducting polymer, by in situ method of synthesis as described in our previous studies (Ashraf et al., 2020, 2023). The surface modified PE described as PE-PANI, was treated with varied concentrations of CD-graphene composite by immersing into the respective CD-graphene solution for overnight. The samples were taken out next day, rinsed with water, allowed to dry in room temperature, noted the initial weight, tagged with numbers to identify the treatments and the fixed over a heavy duty and high density plastic pipe frame. The treatment was PE alone (H0), PE- coated with PANI (PE-PANI), PE-PANI + 0.02 % CD-Graphene, PE-PANI + 0.04 % CD-Graphene, and PE-PANI + 0.08 % CD-Graphene, respectively named as H0, H1, H2, H3, and H4. The samples were exposed 1 m below surface of the estuarine water in the test site of ClFT laboratory at the Cochin estuary. The experiment was initially conducted for all the treatments for 8 months and the best among were selected for further evaluation. The samples were taken after 1, 2, 4, and 8 months and the best among the treatment were selected. The experiment was repeated with 4 replications for upto 4 months and the optimization done based on this result.

The UV–VIS spectra of CD, CD-Graphene in the wavelength range from 190 to 800 nm was obtained using Shimadzu 1450 double beam UV–VIS spectrophotometer. The fluorescent spectral analysis was carried out using Shimadzu RF5301 spectrofluorometer in the excitation wavelength (λex) range of 220–400 nm at 20 nm intervals and emission (λem) range was recorded from 200 to 600 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the materials were analysed using Thermo Nicolet iS10 FTIR fitted with Zn-Se iTR accessory, in the wavenumber of 650–4000 cm−1. The data of the samples were processed using OMNIC software. Bruker D8 powder XRD equipped with Cu Kα radiation at 40 kV was used for X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies with scan rate of 2° min−1. Very dilute solutions of nano materials were dispersed over copper grid and the same was analysed using JEOL JEM-2100F TEM at 200 kV for obtaining surface morphology. Mettler Toledo DSC822e calorimeter and Malvern Mastersizer 3000 particle size behaviour were used for differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and particle size. Park System XE 100 at. force microscope (AFM) fitted with silicon probe having < 10 nm tip in non-contact mode was used for measuring topographical features of the samples. XEI software was used for data processing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CD and its characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: The fish eye contains various pigments, collagen fibers, and chromophores, each serving specific functions in the eye. Rhodopsin and porphyropsin are the two types of visual pigments present in the fish eye. Methionine rich γM-crystallin is found in the lens along with three types of collagen fibers (type I) (Greiling and Clark, 2008). Carbon dots were synthesized by utilizing the above said valuable organics, which generally goes as waste, through hydrothermal carbonization. The pigments, fibers and chromophores present in the fish eye underwent molecular rearrangement in presence of acetic acid and a brown colored fluorescent solution was formed under UV irradiation. UV–VIS spectrum of fish eye CD is shown in Fig. 1a. The absorption maxima were exhibited at 229 and 267 nm and the former absorption was due to the π-π* transition by the conjugated CC core, while the latter by n –π* transition from CO, OH, and C-N groups. The absorption maxima showed by the fish eye CD were comparable to those previously observed for carbon dots derived from fish waste (Keerthana and Ashraf, 2020). The high intensity of absorption and green emission stemmed from the conjugated carbon core, whereas the low intensity absorption originated from functional groups present on the surface (Yu et al., 2012). The high intensity absorption in the UV region indicated the presence of fluorophores in the carbon dot.

The fluorescence spectra of fish eye CD was shown in Fig. 1b. The sample was subjected to excitation from 220 to 400 nm at 20 nm intervals, and their emission spectra were recorded from 200 to 600 nm. When the CD was excited from 220–400 nm wavelength, the emission spectra (λem) maxima varied from 431 to 466 nm with the highest λem maximum at 466 nm observed at an excitation wavelength (λex) of 400 nm. The pattern of emission wavelength shift was recorded as a) λex 240–270 nm exhibited red shift of λem and b) λex 280–320 nm the λem maxima shifted towards blue region (446–431 nm). With further increase in λex from 340 to 400 nm, the λem maxima shifted towards red region, reaching a maximum (466 nm). The intensity of emission spectra increased with increase of λex upto 360 nm except at λex 300 nm, and further increase of λex exhibited a decrease in trend of intensity.

Shifting of λem from blue to red region with increasing the λex indicated the photoluminescence of prepared CD, which depended excitation wavelength (Jing et al., 2019). The emission spectra of the CD with λem maxima confined to a narrow gap of 431–466 nm indicated that the emission was independent of excitation wavelength. This shows the purity, lower size of the CD and also highlighted its use as quantum dots in place of inorganic quantum dots (Sun et al., 2014; Sun et al., 2015). A series of excitation independent-colored CDs with λem maximum were prepared by Wang et al. (Wang et al., 2017). The CD exhibited a narrow range about 36 nm of emission maximum, and slight variation between the excitation lines were due to the influence of naturally present complex molecules or proteins or peptides in the fish eye. The photoluminescence behavior based on excitation was explained due to the excitation of carbons, conjugated carbons, free irregular structures, and surface states (Jing et al., 2019). Narrow photoluminescence emission range in the present study was an indication of smaller size of the CD because of quantum effects and surface containing emission traps (Park et al., 2014). Blue shift was mainly caused in molecules having higher amounts of oxygen functional groups (Jing et al., 2019). The fluorescence of carbon dot was mainly contributed by the λem exhibited at λex 220–270 nm and 320–360 nm. The former was due to conjugated system and the latter was from surface groups of CD. This further underlined the secondary absorption maxima exhibited around λex 220 and 320 nm which appeared as shoulder in the spectra. Carbon dot was mainly graphitic carbon core with a surface rich in OH, COO- and CO functional groups and these functional groups undergo protonation under acidic environment (Liu et al., 2021). Intra or inter molecular hydrogen bonding in the CD surface resulted in strong vibrational coupling of the surface OH groups, as indicated by broader bands in the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum (Liu et al., 2021). In general, the carbon dots derived from fish eye exhibited a strong and narrow fluorescence range that was independent of excitation. Moreover, they were smaller in size, rich in conjugated CC bonds, and exhibited high purity.

The FTIR spectra of fish eye CD is presented in Fig. 2 with detailed functional group information provided in Table 1. The primary amine, O-H (in 2 environments) and CC vibrations respectively at 1588, 1402 + 1244 and 1640 cm−1 exhibited with intense absorption indicated the strong presence of these functional group in the CD. The OH groups in the CD were in primary and tertiary orientation. The small shoulders at 3073 and 1517 cm−1 was indicative of low amounts of aromatic CC-H stretching. The wider peak from 3200 to 3570 cm−1 showed the presence of hydrogen bonded O-H group and this was further evident by the presence of a shoulder at 1660 cm−1. The OH groups are in three different environments as evidenced by the absorption peak of 1328, 1373 and 1402 cm−1 pointed as tertiary, carboxyl and primary OH. The NH2 existed as primary amine. The OH functional groups are comparatively higher in the carbon dots, as evidenced by the intensities of the OH bands. This was reflected in the fluorescence spectra, where the emission wavelength (λem) shifted towards the blue region with increased excitation wavelength (λex) from 280 to 320 nm (Ashraf et al., 2021).

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) and transmission electron micrographs (TEM) topography of nano CD was shown in Fig. 3. The TEM indicated a particle size of around 5 nm and the AFM showed a higher size of 64 nm. This may be probably due to the better resolution of TEM compared to the AFM. The AFM measures the diameter of the spherical particles through height dimension and has expected higher aberration effects which may deform laser beam of the cantilever. Another possibility for the variation might be the aggregation of CD. The average roughness (Ra), root mean square roughness (Rq) and peak to valley height (Rpv) respectively was 0.456, 0.555 and 2.653 nm. The data showed that the surface moderately rough and the skewness (Rsk) (-0.756) and kurtosis (Rku) (3.633) confirmed normal height distribution implying improved surface interactions and applications of CD (Supplementary file Fig S1).

The zeta potential exhibited by the carbon dot was + 30.1 mV and the positive value might be due to the protonated carboxyl group and hydrogen bonding of CD in acidic environment (Clogston and Patri, 2010; Liu et al., 2021). The zeta potential values suggested the good stability and improved electrostatic interaction with negatively charged species; this will enable to form composites easily with graphene.

Synthesis of CD - graphene was carried out by employing different ratios of CD and graphene through hydrothermal method. The formed composites were evaluated using spectrophotometer and the optimum concentration was 1:3 CD and graphene. The samples were stored in a desiccator for further evaluation

TEM and AFM micrographs of CD-graphene hybrid was shown in Fig. 4. The size of the CD-graphene particles was around 21 nm and that of AFM was 65 nm. As explained earlier the size variation in AFM evaluation may probably due to the aggregation during sample processing. The SAED pattern showed that material is amorphous in nature.

The FTIR absorption characteristics of CD – graphene composite was evaluated and shown in Fig. 5 and Table 1. Introduction of graphene in CD has highly influenced the IR absorption characteristics. Significant changes were recorded in the C-H stretching vibrations of conjugated CC system, and functional groups like CH3, O-H and N-H (Table 1). This indicated that hydrothermal method of synthesis enabled a strong interaction between the CD and graphene. The FTIR characteristics clearly established the formation of the composite and the strong interaction between the conjugated CC bonds, O-H. and N-H functional groups. The changes exhibited in CH3, CH etc were probably due to the inductive or resonance or steric influences.

The XRD patterns of CD-graphene composite is shown in Fig. 5b. Primarily, four diffraction peaks were exhibited at 26.40, 42.67, 44.00 and nick at 24.06°. Among these the intense diffraction peak at 2θ= 26.4° (002) is characteristic of graphene. The nick along with intense peak at 24.06, 42.67 (100) and 44.00° (101) are characteristic signature of carbon dots and the results are comparable to the findings of Kumar and Ojha (2016). The expected sharp peak of the CD around 2θ= 20° (Bajpai et al., 2019) was observed with a slight shift, due to the formation of the composite. Similarly, the less intense broad peak was exhibited around 2θ = 43° also indicated the contribution of CD in the composite (Kumar et al., 2017). The graphene oxide also exhibited low intense multiple peaks around 2θ = 43° and it was explained due to the oxygen in the graphene oxide (Kumar et al., 2017). The present study the peak shown was probably due to the interaction of graphene with OH of CD. The wide d-spacing at 2θ= 26° (002) highlighted the presence of oxygen in the matrix (Kumar et al., 2017), which was originated from the CD or acquired during the composite formation. The CD was comprised of a conjugated double bond system with surfaces containing varied functional groups and the composite of CD-graphene was probably formed in layers. The broad nature of diffraction peak around 26° inferred the formation of nano scale sized composite with few unorganised layers (Kumar and Ojha, 2016) and amorphous nature (Bajpai et al., 2019). The XRD pattern indicated the formation of CD- graphene composite with layered structure.

CDs were formed during hydrothermal carbonization by undergoing structural reorientation, cycization of conjugated CC rich visual pigments like rhodopsin and porphyropsin present in the fish eye and other amino acids. These molecules during hydrothermal process might have formed aromatic clusters containing graphitic carbon core, after dehydration, polymerisation and defect formation (Roy et al., 2015). The NH, CO, OH, and COOH functional groups in the CD surface enhanced hydrophilicity, stability, and fluorescence of excitation independent emission.

The polyethylene aquaculture cage nets of specified size were taken and the surface was modified by in situ synthesizing polyaniline over cage net as described in the materials and method section. The surface modified PE, described as PE-PANI, was treated with varied concentrations of CD-graphene composite. The pre-weighed CD-graphene coated cage net samples were exposed 1 m below the tide water level in the test site of ICAR Central Institute of Fisheries Technology at the Cochin estuary and samples were drawn after, 1, 2, 4, and 8 months (December 14, 2022 to August 17, 2023). After an exposure period of one month in the estuary, untreated control sample exhibited higher biomass accumulation than CD-graphene treated samples (Supplementary file Figure S2). Second month samples were accumulated with exotic bivalve, Mytilla strigata and it was highest in the H0, untreated net. The PVC frame rods were infested heavily with Mytilidae, extended the infestation towards the net, resulting in accumulation primarily on the sides of the net that were in contact with the PVC rod. Infestation of bivalves on the net did not show any discrimination among treatments, but minimal accumulation was observed in the treated ones. The presence of exotic organism Mytilla strigata were reported a few years back in the region and spontaneous eruptions were shown during the period of October to April (Jayachandran et al., 2019). Untreated control and PE-PANI treated net exhibited highest biofouling density in samples retrieved after 4, 6, and 8 months. The sampling period for the 1 and 2 month sample was during the post monsoon period, the 4 month sample was during summer or pre monsoon period, and the 6 and 8 month samples were retrieved during monsoon period. The varied levels of fouling density observed among the samples from different months were attributed to the influence of the southwest monsoon rain and other prevailing weather conditions in the region. Variations in fouling pattern were expected since the cage net experienced varied salinity regime due to seasons. Comparatively stable behavior with lowest biofouling accumulation was exhibited by the cage net treated with 0.02 % CD-graphene. Higher concentrations of CD- graphene exhibited lower biofouling resistance may be probably due to the multiple layers and their inability to participate in the photocatalytic reactions. Samples drawn post-monsoon exhibited comparatively higher biofouler accumulation, followed by those collected during the pre-monsoon period. During monsoon season all samples exhibited lower fouling density and it was mainly due to the sloughing off of the foulers due to the fresh water inflow, lower atmospheric temperature and low salinity due to dilution (Dong et al., 2020). The cage net clearly showed varied diversity of biofouling organism with seasonal variation. The fouler diversity was not analysed in the present study. The results showed treatment of composite had a significant influence on the accumulation of biofouling organisms. Post monsoon was the period having higher productivity in the region and hence higher biofouling accumulation (Pham et al., 2015). Recent incidents of spontaneous Mytilidae attacks on aquaculture cage nets obscure the presence of other regular foulers. It should also be noted that the biocide used in the present study was specifically designed to target regular biofoulers. It is an urgent need to develop strategies to mitigate both exotic bivalve like Mytilidae and regular biofoulers. Bivalve infestations, such as those by Mytilidae, were spontaneous and massive in nature. As a result, fishers were forced to cease their aquaculture operations due to the heavy deformation and weight experienced by the cage nets. The results highlighted the treatments with 0.02 % (H2) and 0.04 % (H3) CD-graphene has higher biofouling inhibition and the same experiments were repeated with the treatments H0, H2 and H3 with more replications.

The field experiments clearly demonstrated that treatments H2 and H3 performed well, prompting the conduction of a follow-up experiment to confirm the repeatability of the results. The field exposure experiment was repeated with untreated H0, H2 and H3 treated samples with more replications and the results are shown in Fig. 6. The experiment was conducted from September 27th, 2023 to February 2nd, 2024 and the period comes under post monsoon season. During 4 months exposure the treatment H2 exhibited highest biofouling resistance compared to H3 and control. After 1 and 2 month H3 sample exhibited comparatively higher biofouling than H2 and H0, whereas after 4 months H2 exhibited lowest biofouling incidence. During the initial two months, the region experienced intermittent monsoon rain which resulted in low salinity level in the estuary, hence lower biofouler accumulation during this period. This showed that 0.02 % of CD-graphene hybrid was ideal for optimum performance. The higher biofouling observed with the increasing concentration of CD-graphene might be due to the greater number of layers of the hybrid over the PANI. This could potentially reduce the efficiency of electron transmission within the polyaniline layer, thereby leading to increased microbial activity around the cage net.

The SEM of CD-graphene treated PE-PANI net was shown in Fig. 7. The sphere shaped CD-graphene was clearly seen over the net and it was distributed uniformly. The untreated PE and PE-PANI nets SEM images were not shown here since it was already reported in our earlier publications (Ashraf et al., 2020). The micrographs showed that the CD were enveloped the graphene and firmly rooted over the polyaniline surface.

CDs are generally non-toxic in vivo and in vitro environment, and benevolent in nature. It has excellent light harvesting power in the UV to IR range of electromagnetic spectrum and strong photodynamic effects (Ge et al., 2016; Lan et al., 2018). CD can produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) during photoexcitation and it was toxic to microorganisms. Interaction of CD with the bacterial surface in presence of light will initialise the ROS production, produce free radical (OH.) and/or singlet oxygen, slowly disrupt and damage the cell wall function, induce oxidative stress to the DNA/RNA, create an erratic gene expression signals, and finally oxidative damages to proteins and other biomolecular functions (Ipe et al., 2005; Li et al., 2016; Awak et al., 2017; Jhonsi et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018). The process of ROS induced cell death was initialized by protein deactivation, peroxidation of lipids, erratic function of mitochondria, gradual collapse of cell membrane, finally death of cell due to necrosis/apoptosis (Dong et al., 2020). Mechanism of ROS species generation was not well understood

The mechanism of antibacterial property of graphene was explained on creating oxidative stress on membrane, electron transfer and damage due to sharp edge during physical contact (Pinto et al., 2013; Smith et al., 2015). Oxidative stress was the major mechanism at play, as inactivation of proteins and lipids occurred due to the reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Smith et al., 2015). Microbial inhibition by electron transfer was occurred by abstraction of electron from cell membrane by graphene, which in turn damage the integrity of cell membrane (Chen et al., 2015). Tu et al. (2013) reported that nano graphene layers can enter the cell membrane and extract phospholipids leading to cell death. Pham et al. (2015) reported that antimicrobial activity of graphene depends on the graphene edge density.

The CD-graphene hybrid possessed both electron-withdrawing and electron-releasing functional groups, leading to notable conductivity changes, attributed to the conical structure of graphene (Alzate et al., 2020). It is reported that hybrids of CD with porphyrins, perylenediimides and extended tetrathiafulvalene exhibited photon induced charge transfer mechanism and in all the cases CDs acted as either electron donor or acceptor (Strauss et al., 2015; Scharl et al., 2018). Similar mechanisms were observed in the case of hybrids with exfoliated MoS2 and WS2 dichalcogenides (Li et al., 2016). There is not much reported on the antimicrobial characteristics of CD-graphene composites and their application as biocides to combat biofouling. Hybrids synthesized with CD-spermidine exhibited the antibacterial properties against E. coli, S. aureus, B. subtilis, and P. aeruginosa and methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA) (Li et al., 2016). The positively charged CD-spermidine interacted with negatively charged cell membrane of microorganism by binding with nucleic acids, protein etc., without forming intracellular ROS (Dong et al., 2020). Hybrids of CD with ampicillin, exhibited not only excellent antibacterial effects against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria but also generated reactive oxygen species (ROS) in presence of visible light. They required reduced amounts of biocides and demonstrated inhibition against various microorganisms like E coli (Jijie et al., 2018). In the present study, under visible light, the hybrids might have produced reactive oxygen species (ROS), leading to an increased presence of free radicals and thereby creating a higher acidic environment in the vicinity of the cage net due to the formation of H2O2 (Nakai and Tsuruta, 2021). Increased production of ROS implied that the system is under higher oxidative stress (Nakai and Tsuruta, 2021). Also the increased electron transition between the CD-graphene interface lead to higher activity around the cage net. The electron transfer from CD-graphene to underlying conducting polyaniline layer might have further induced higher electron generation and transfer, hence increased antibacterial property. This slowed down the accumulation of microorganisms around the netting materials and hence biofouling resistance. The CD-graphene and polyaniline played synergistically to create a biofouling resistance environment. The emission spectra of CD was excitation dependent and multi chromophore presence attributed to different functional groups on the surface also synergistically influenced the production of ROS species and hence biofouling resistance (Gao et al., 2017; Shabbir, 2023)

Carbon dot was synthesized from rohu fish eye through hydrothermal method and its spectral characterization revealed the conjugated CC bonds and other functional groups, which are respectively responsible for π-π* and n-π* transitions. Fluorescence spectral characteristics revealed characteristics emission spectra responsible for π-π* and n-π* transitions, excitation independent emission spectrum, smaller size of the particle and purity of the material. Varied concentrations of hydrothermally synthesized CD-graphene hybrid was coated over PANI surface modified polyethylene aquaculture cage nets and exposed the treated cage nets in the marine environments for 8 months to study the biofouling resistance. The study highlighted 0.02 % CD-graphene hybrid coated PE-PANI aquaculture cage nets with maximum biofouling inhibition. The probable mechanism of the CD-graphene hybrid as a biocide was the synergism in electron transmission under sunlight, enhancing the overall production of ROS species and free radicals. The formation of peroxide created an acidic environment in the vicinity of the cage net, deterring microbial attachment to the net. This delayed the accumulation of biofoulers. Additionally, utilization of fish waste to create high value products like carbon dot enable to mitigate environmental pollution and the prepared CD-graphene hybrid was a potential candidate as biocide against biofouling in aquaculture cage nets. Monitoring of CD-graphene degradation and its presence in the aquatic system due to leaching needs more research. The research needs to identify the degradation products of carbon dot and graphene, and their quantification. More emphasis required to enhance the durability of the coated biocide, its safety in the environment, molecular level mechanism of biofouling inhibition and commercialization.

P Muhamed Ashraf: Conceptualization and Planning, writing of the manuscript, and data interpretation. Sahla VA and Anjana CS: application of biocide over the aqualcuture cage net, Instrumental analysis, field evaluation and data compilation. Binsi PK, Conceptualization and Planning, writing of the manuscript, and data interpretation.

Ashraf P. Muhamed: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. P K Binsi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. V A Sahla: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. C S Anjana: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis.

“The Corresponding Author declares that this manuscript is original, has not been published before, and is not currently being considered for publication elsewhere. All authors have contributed significantly to the research and development of this manuscript and have incorporated all the corrections and suggestions raised by respected reviewers.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
No software used for coding.
ICAR Central Institute of Fisheries Technology, Cochin, India, approved the manuscript for publication with reference No RPP/0XX/2024.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors sincerely thank the Director, ICAR- Central Institute of Fisheries Technology and Head of Fishing Technology, and Microbiology, Fermentation and Biotechnology Division for providing facilities.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.aquaeng.2025.102586.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanodots: supramolecular electron donor–acceptor hybrids featuring perylenediimides

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Volker Strauss, Johannes T. Margraf, Konstantin Dirian, Zois Syrgiannis, Maurizio Prato, Cordula Wessendorf, Andreas Hirsch,. Timothy Clark, Dirk M. Guldi. "Carbon nanodots: supramolecular electron donor–acceptor hybrids featuring perylenediimides." Angewandte Chemie International Edition 54(28) (2015): 8292-8297. https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201502482

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Sun et al., 2015

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Combination of carbon dot and polymer dot phosphors for white light-emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sun, C., Zhang, Y., Sun, K., Reckmeier, C., Zhang, T., Zhang, X., Zhao, J., Wu, C., Yu, W.W., Rogach, A.L., 2015. Combination of carbon dot and polymer dot phosphors for white light-emitting diodes. Nanoscale, 7(28),12045-12050. https://doi.org/10.1039/C5NR03014E.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Sun et al., 2014

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High color rendering index white light emitting diodes fabricated from a combination of carbon dots and zinc copper indium sulfide quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sun, C., Zhang, Y., Wang, Y., Liu, W., Kalytchuk, S., Kershaw, S.V., Zhang, T., Zhang, X., Zhao, J., Yu, W.W., Rogach, A.L., 2014. High color rendering index white light emitting diodes fabricated from a combination of carbon dots and zinc copper indium sulfide quantum dots. Applied Physics Letters, 104, 261106. https://doi.org/10.1063/1.4886415

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Destructive extraction of phospholipids from Escherichia coli membranes by graphene nanosheets

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Excitation wavelength independent visible color emission of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent carbon dots as an efficient nanoprobe for detection of clomifene citrate
